subject: [RELEASE] Post-Release Report v4.1.8 – Metrics, Lessons Learned & Retro
body:
Hi Team,

Post-Release Report: v4.1.8 (Released: 4th October 2024)
Author: Fish John | Written: 21st October 2024 (2-week retrospective)

Release Summary:
Services deployed: 5 | Duration: 1h 45min | Outcome: SUCCESS with minor incident

What Went Well:
✅ Zero-downtime deployment achieved using rolling update strategy
✅ All database migrations backward compatible — no schema lock issues
✅ Canary deployment for API gateway caught a configuration issue at 10% traffic
   (would have affected 100% of users if full deploy — canary strategy WORKS)
✅ Automated rollback tested and confirmed working in staging

What Went Wrong:
⚠️ INCIDENT: notification-service had elevated error rate (3.2%) for 12 minutes post-deploy
   Root cause: New RabbitMQ connection pool settings too conservative — thread starvation
   Fix: Increased connection pool from 10 to 50. Resolved without rollback.
   
⚠️ Deployment started 35 minutes late (6:35 PM vs 6:00 PM)
   Cause: On-call handoff documentation was incomplete. New on-call engineer (Rohit Singh)
   took 30 min to find runbook. Runbook location moved but Confluence link not updated.

Metrics:
Deploy frequency: 2 major releases + 1 hotfix in October ✅ (DORA: High)
Lead time for changes: 4.2 days (target: < 5 days) ✅
Change failure rate: 1/3 releases needed hotfix = 33% (target: < 15%) ❌ NEEDS IMPROVEMENT
MTTR (hotfix): 2h 15min (target: < 4 hours) ✅

Action Items from Retro:
1. Fix all runbook links in Confluence (Owner: Fish John, Due: 25th Oct)
2. Add RabbitMQ connection pool to release checklist validation
3. Set change failure rate target review: root cause is race condition in new UPI code — process fix: mandatory concurrent load test for payment features

Fish John
Release Manager | DevOps Lead

subject: [L1-TKT-1001] Jenkins pipeline failing – Build not triggering on Git push
body:
Hi Team,

Ticket: L1-TKT-1001 | Priority: Low | Raised by: Dev Team (Ravi Patel)
Environment: Development | Component: Jenkins CI

Issue Description:
The Jenkins pipeline for repository 'backend-api' is not triggering automatically when developers push code to the 'develop' branch. Manual builds work fine but webhook-based auto-trigger is broken.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Push any commit to develop branch in GitLab
2. Expected: Jenkins pipeline auto-triggers within 60 seconds
3. Actual: No build triggered. Manual trigger from Jenkins UI works.

Basic Checks Done:
- Jenkins service is running (confirmed via systemctl status jenkins)
- GitLab webhook URL configured: http://jenkins.internal:8080/gitlab/build_now
- No error visible in Jenkins logs at surface level

Action Taken:
Checked GitLab webhook settings. Last delivery shows HTTP 403 error.
Likely cause: Jenkins CSRF token mismatch or API token expired.

Resolution Steps (L1):
Please verify:
1. GitLab webhook token matches Jenkins API token
2. CSRF protection settings in Jenkins → Manage Jenkins → Security

If unresolved, escalating to L2 after 4 hours.

Fish John
L1 DevOps Support | Ticket Auto-close: 48 hrs if no response

subject: [RELEASE] Hotfix Release v4.2.1 – Critical Payment Gateway Bug
body:
URGENT – Hotfix Release v4.2.1

Release Manager: Fish John | Date: Sunday 20th October 2024, 11:30 PM IST
Triggered by: P1 Incident INC-2024-445

Incident Summary:
Post v4.2.0 release, UPI payment success rate dropped to 67% (expected: 99.5%)
Root cause: payment-service v2.9.0 has a race condition in UPI callback handler
affecting transactions > ₹10,000. Discovered at 10:45 PM by on-call monitoring.

Impact:
- 847 failed UPI transactions since 6 PM (18th Oct)
- Estimated customer impact: ₹2.4 crore in failed transactions
- Transactions not lost (idempotency keys working) but customers seeing failure UI

Hotfix Details (payment-service v2.9.1):
Bug: UPI callback webhook handler not thread-safe for concurrent callbacks on same transaction ID
Fix: Added ReentrantLock on transaction processing in UpiCallbackService.java
Fix size: 23 lines changed | PR: #4521 | Reviewed by: Vikram Nair (emergency review)

Expedited Testing (90 minutes):
22:00 – Fix committed and reviewed
22:15 – Unit tests: 847 tests, 0 failures ✅
22:30 – Integration test: UPI simulator — 500 concurrent transactions > ₹10,000 ✅
22:45 – Security review: no new vulnerabilities introduced ✅
23:00 – Deployed to staging: 100% success rate in 15-min soak ✅

Deployment (23:30 PM – 23:50 PM):
23:30 – Deployed payment-service v2.9.1 via ArgoCD (rolling, zero downtime)
23:42 – All pods running new version
23:45 – UPI success rate: 99.7% ✅ Incident resolved.

Retrospective: Monday 21st Oct, 10 AM
Customer refund/retry process initiated for 847 affected transactions.

Fish John
Release Manager + L3 Backend

subject: [L1-TKT-1002] Docker container not starting – Port 8080 already in use
body:
Hi,

Ticket: L1-TKT-1002 | Priority: Low | Raised by: QA Team (Sneha Roy)
Environment: QA Server (qa-server-02) | Component: Docker

Issue:
Docker container 'payment-service' failing to start on qa-server-02.
Error: "Error starting userland proxy: listen tcp 0.0.0.0:8080: bind: address already in use"

Investigation:
Ran: sudo netstat -tulpn | grep 8080
Output shows: java process PID 23421 is holding port 8080 — this is a zombie process from a previous failed deployment.

Root Cause: Previous Jenkins deployment job crashed mid-way leaving orphan Java process running.

Fix Applied:
1. sudo kill -9 23421
2. docker start payment-service
3. Container started successfully. Health check passing.

Status: RESOLVED ✅
Time to Resolve: 18 minutes
No escalation needed.

Please ensure deployment scripts include port cleanup step before container start.
Created Improvement Request: IR-2024-089

Fish John
L1 DevOps Support

subject: [L1-TKT-1003] SonarQube quality gate failing – Code coverage below threshold
body:
Hi Team,

Ticket: L1-TKT-1003 | Priority: Low | Raised by: Dev Team (Arjun Mehta)
Environment: CI Pipeline | Component: SonarQube 9.9 LTS

Issue:
CI pipeline failing at SonarQube quality gate step for project 'user-service'.
Error: "Quality gate status: FAILED – Coverage: 58.2% (threshold: 80%)"

Context:
New feature branch 'feature/payment-refactor' merged 2 new service classes without unit tests.
Code coverage dropped from 84% to 58.2%.

This is NOT a tool failure — this is a code quality issue.

Action:
1. Informed dev lead Arjun Mehta that pipeline will remain blocked until coverage meets threshold.
2. Quality gate is working as designed — this is expected behaviour.
3. Dev team must add unit tests before merge is allowed.

Recommendation:
- Add pre-commit hooks to warn developers when coverage drops locally
- Consider reducing threshold temporarily to 70% during refactor (requires manager approval)

Status: PENDING DEV ACTION – Ticket assigned to Arjun Mehta
SLA: Dev team to resolve within 1 business day.

Fish John
L1 DevOps Support

subject: [L1-TKT-1004] Kubernetes pod CrashLoopBackOff – Missing environment variable
body:
Hi,

Ticket: L1-TKT-1004 | Priority: Medium | Raised by: Backend Team
Environment: Staging (k8s-staging-cluster) | Component: Kubernetes

Issue:
Pod 'notification-service-6d8f9b' in namespace 'staging' stuck in CrashLoopBackOff.

kubectl describe pod notification-service-6d8f9b output:
Back-off restarting failed container
Last Exit Code: 1

kubectl logs notification-service-6d8f9b:
Error: Environment variable 'SMTP_HOST' not found. Application cannot start.

Root Cause:
ConfigMap 'notification-config' was not updated when the new SMTP server was migrated from smtp.internal to smtp.acmecorp.com last week (Change Request CR-2024-112).
The new env variable was added to production ConfigMap but missed in staging.

Fix:
kubectl edit configmap notification-config -n staging
Added: SMTP_HOST=smtp.acmecorp.com

kubectl rollout restart deployment notification-service -n staging
Pod now Running. All 3 replicas healthy.

Status: RESOLVED ✅ | Time to Resolve: 25 minutes

Lesson Learned: ConfigMap changes must be applied to ALL environments simultaneously.
Added to runbook: https://confluence.internal/devops/runbooks/configmap-sync

Fish John
L1 Support | Kubernetes Team

subject: [L1-TKT-1005] Nexus Repository unreachable – Dependency download failing in pipeline
body:
Hi Team,

Ticket: L1-TKT-1005 | Priority: Medium | Raised by: All Dev Teams (multiple reporters)
Environment: All CI Pipelines | Component: Nexus Repository Manager 3.x

Issue:
All Maven/Gradle builds failing since 09:15 AM with:
Could not resolve com.acme:shared-utils:2.1.4
Connection refused: nexus.internal:8081

Impact: ALL pipelines blocked. ~15 active builds failing simultaneously.

Investigation:
ping nexus.internal → Request timeout (server not responding)
SSH to nexus-server-01: Connection timed out

Escalated to Infrastructure team: Server appears to be down.
Infra response: nexus-server-01 ran out of disk space (100% utilisation on /data partition).
Nexus auto-shutdown triggered when disk full.

Immediate Fix by Infra (Vikram Nair):
1. Deleted old blob store snapshots (freed 120GB)
2. Restarted Nexus service: systemctl restart nexus
3. Service online at 10:45 AM

All pipelines resumed. 15 failed builds re-triggered manually.

Long-term Fix:
- Set up disk usage alert at 80% threshold (PagerDuty)
- Scheduled weekly blob store cleanup cron job

Status: RESOLVED ✅ | Downtime: 1.5 hours | Impact: High

Fish John
L1 DevOps Support

subject: [MAINT] Monthly Kubernetes Node Patch – OS Security Updates + Kernel Upgrade Oct 2024
body:
Hi Team,

Maintenance Window: CR-2024-188 | Monthly Node Patching
Date: Sunday 20th October 2024 | 1:00 AM – 5:00 AM IST
Scope: All 12 EKS worker nodes — OS patches + kernel upgrade

Patches Applied:
- Kernel: 5.10.190 → 5.10.226 (CVE-2024-39480 kernel privilege escalation fix)
- glibc: 2.31-13 → 2.31-15 (CVE-2024-2961 buffer overflow fix)
- openssl: 3.0.2 → 3.0.15 (multiple CVE fixes including CVE-2024-45231)
- containerd: 1.7.8 → 1.7.13 (CVE-2024-21626 container escape fix — CRITICAL)
- AWS SSM Agent: 3.2.1630 → 3.3.24

Patching Procedure (rolling — zero downtime):
For each node:
1. kubectl cordon <node> — prevent new pod scheduling
2. kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data — move existing pods
3. AWS SSM Run Command: patch + reboot
4. Wait for node Ready status (avg 3 min 40 sec)
5. kubectl uncordon <node> — return to service

Results:
Node 01/12: ✅ 4m12s | Node 02/12: ✅ 3m58s | Node 03/12: ✅ 4m05s
Node 04/12: ✅ 3m44s | Node 05/12: ✅ 4m01s | Node 06/12: ⚠️ 8m12s (slow reboot — investigated, BIOS POST delay, normal)
Node 07/12: ✅ 3m55s | Node 08/12: ✅ 4m10s | Node 09/12: ✅ 3m48s
Node 10/12: ✅ 4m02s | Node 11/12: ✅ 3m59s | Node 12/12: ✅ 4m15s

Total duration: 58 minutes | Zero pod disruption confirmed.
containerd escape CVE-2024-21626 confirmed patched — critical security fix.

Next maintenance: Sunday 17th November 2024.

Fish John
L2 Infrastructure Maintenance

subject: [L2-TKT-2001] ESCALATION – Jenkins shared library throwing ClassNotFoundException in all pipelines
body:
Hi Senior DevOps Team,

Ticket: L2-TKT-2001 | Priority: High | Escalated from: L1-TKT-1008
Environment: Production Jenkins | Component: Jenkins Shared Library (Groovy)

Escalation Reason:
L1 team unable to resolve after 4 hours. Issue affecting ALL 47 active pipelines.

Error (from pipeline logs):
java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: com.acme.pipeline.utils.DockerUtils
    at groovy.lang.GroovyClassLoader.loadClass(GroovyClassLoader.java:693)
    at org.jenkinsci.plugins.workflow.cps.CpsFlowExecution.loadClass

Background:
This started after a shared library update was pushed to GitLab branch 'main' at 11:30 PM last night by developer Kiran Desai. The commit updated DockerUtils.groovy — class signature changed, breaking all downstream pipeline imports.

Root Cause Analysis (L2):
1. Class 'DockerUtils' had method signature change: buildImage(String) → buildImage(String, Map)
2. All Jenkinsfile scripts calling old signature: DockerUtils.buildImage(imageName)
3. No backward compatibility maintained in library update
4. No staging test of shared library before merge to main

Immediate Fix Options:
Option A: Revert shared library commit (git revert) — fastest, 10 min downtime
Option B: Update all 47 Jenkinsfiles to new signature — 3-4 hours work
Option C: Add overloaded method in DockerUtils to support both signatures — 30 min

Recommendation: Option C (backward compatible fix)
Implementing now. ETA: 30 minutes.

Fish John
L2 DevOps | Senior Pipeline Engineer

00:25 – 16.3 → 16.4: apt-get install gitlab-ce=16.4.7-ce.0 — ✅ 8 min
00:35 – 16.4 → 16.5: ✅ 7 min (DB migration: added index on merge_request_metrics)
00:44 – 16.5 → 16.6: ✅ 9 min
00:55 – 16.6 → 16.7: ✅ 8 min
01:05 – 16.7 → 16.8.1: ✅ 11 min (background migration: 2.1M rows in ci_builds — ran async)

subject: [L2-TKT-2002] ArgoCD sync failing – Helm chart values mismatch between Git and cluster
body:
Hi Team,

Ticket: L2-TKT-2002 | Priority: High | Component: ArgoCD + Helm
Environment: Production Kubernetes (prod-cluster-01)

Issue:
ArgoCD application 'payments-app' stuck in OutOfSync state for 36 hours despite manual sync attempts.

ArgoCD Sync Error:
ComparisonError: rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Helm template
failed: Error: values don't meet the specifications of the schema in values.schema.json:
  - (root): Additional property 'replicaOverride' is not allowed

Investigation:
1. Git repo has values.schema.json with strict JSON schema validation (added in last Helm chart version bump 3.1.0 → 3.2.0)
2. Our values-production.yaml was passing 'replicaOverride' key — valid in 3.1.0 but removed from schema in 3.2.0
3. ArgoCD upgraded its Helm version from 3.10 to 3.13 last week which now enforces schema validation strictly

Root Cause: Helm chart version upgrade introduced breaking schema change. Our production values file was not updated.

Fix:
1. Removed 'replicaOverride' from values-production.yaml (functionality moved to autoscaling.enabled: true)
2. Validated locally: helm template --values values-production.yaml . — no errors
3. ArgoCD sync succeeded. All 6 pods healthy.

Preventive Action:
Added helm lint step in the values-file PR pipeline to catch schema violations before merge.

Status: RESOLVED ✅

Fish John
L2 GitOps / ArgoCD Engineer

subject: [MAINT] Jenkins Disk Cleanup – Build Artifacts Purge + Log Rotation Oct 2024
body:
Hi Team,

Maintenance Task: MAINT-2024-077
Date: 21st October 2024
Task: Jenkins build artifact cleanup and disk space reclamation

Background:
Jenkins master disk usage: 847GB / 1TB (85% full)
Alert triggered at 80% threshold (PagerDuty: ALERT-4521)
Without cleanup, Jenkins will hit disk capacity within 5 days.

Analysis:
du -sh /var/lib/jenkins/jobs/*/builds/ | sort -h | tail -20
Top space consumers:
1. backend-api: 124GB (builds retained for 365 days — excessive)
2. data-pipeline: 98GB (artifact archives never cleaned)
3. mobile-app: 76GB (iOS build artifacts: ~500MB each)
4. integration-tests: 67GB (test reports + screenshots accumulating since 2022)

Cleanup Actions:
1. Updated job retention policies:
   - Max builds to keep: 30 (was: unlimited or 365 days)
   - Max artifact size: 500MB per build
   - Discard old builds: after 30 days

2. Ran Jenkins built-in cleanup:
   jenkins-cli.jar build "cleanup-old-builds" — freed 312GB immediately

3. Archived 2022 build logs to S3 (compliance: keep 2 years):
   aws s3 sync /archive/jenkins-2022/ s3://acme-jenkins-archive/2022/

4. Configured log rotation: /etc/logrotate.d/jenkins
   rotate 7 | compress | maxsize 100M

Result:
Before: 847GB (85%)
After: 421GB (42%)
Freed: 426GB ✅

Disk alert cleared. Next full cleanup scheduled: January 2025.

Fish John
L1 Jenkins Maintenance

subject: [L2-TKT-2003] Terraform state file corruption – Plan shows unexpected resource destruction
body:
URGENT – L2-TKT-2003 | Priority: Critical | Component: Terraform / AWS

Team,

STOP ALL TERRAFORM APPLIES IMMEDIATELY.

Situation:
During routine infrastructure change for Project Atlas (adding 2 new EC2 instances), terraform plan showed:
- 14 resources to add ✅ (expected)
- 0 to change ✅ (expected)
- 89 resources to destroy ❌ (UNEXPECTED — this includes production RDS, EKS cluster, VPCs)

This would destroy our entire production AWS infrastructure if applied.

Investigation:
1. Terraform state file (terraform.tfstate) last modified: 2 hours ago by automated backup script
2. Backup script incorrectly wrote a partial state file over the main state file
3. Terraform workspace: prod — state now shows only 14 of 103 expected resources

Remote State:
Confirmed S3 backend has versioned state files. Last good state: S3://acme-tf-state/prod/terraform.tfstate (version: 47, timestamp: 6 hours ago)

Recovery Steps:
1. aws s3api get-object --bucket acme-tf-state --key prod/terraform.tfstate --version-id versionID terraform.tfstate.good
2. terraform state replace with recovered file
3. terraform plan — now shows 0 destroy. ✅

All clear to resume Terraform operations.

Immediate Actions:
- Fixed backup script (was using > instead of cp for state file)
- Added S3 versioning alert if state file size drops >50%
- Added mandatory plan review gate before apply for destroy count > 0

Fish John
L2 Infrastructure / Terraform Lead

subject: [L2-TKT-2004] Prometheus alerting rules not firing – AlertManager misconfiguration
body:
Hi Monitoring Team,

Ticket: L2-TKT-2004 | Priority: High | Component: Prometheus + AlertManager
Environment: Production Monitoring Stack

Problem:
Production incident last Friday (P1 – database connection pool exhaustion) was NOT alerted by our monitoring.
Alert rule 'DatabaseConnectionPoolHigh' should have fired when pool utilisation exceeded 85%.
Manual monitoring caught the issue 2 hours late, causing service degradation.

L2 Investigation:
1. Checked Prometheus UI → Alert Rules: 'DatabaseConnectionPoolHigh' showing state: INACTIVE
2. Rule definition in prometheus-rules.yaml:
   expr: db_connection_pool_used / db_connection_pool_max > 0.85
   
3. Checked metric name: actual metric in Prometheus is 'database_pool_used_connections' NOT 'db_connection_pool_used'
   Metric name changed when we upgraded the DB exporter from v0.11 to v0.14 last month.
   
4. AlertManager routing config had a duplicate receiver block causing silencing of 'database' category alerts.

Fixes Applied:
1. Updated prometheus-rules.yaml with correct metric names
2. Fixed AlertManager routing — removed duplicate receiver
3. Added Prometheus rule unit tests (promtool test rules)
4. Tested alert firing with: promtool query instant http://prometheus:9090 'database_pool_used_connections'

Alert now firing correctly in test. Deployed to production.

Post-Mortem scheduled: Monday 10 AM.

Fish John
L2 Observability Engineer

subject: [L3-TKT-3001] CRITICAL – Kubernetes cluster node NotReady causing production pod evictions
body:
P0 INCIDENT – L3-TKT-3001 | Component: Kubernetes Cluster / kubelet
Environment: Production EKS Cluster (us-east-1) | Escalated from L2-TKT-2009

INCIDENT ACTIVE – Started 14:22 IST

Situation:
3 of 12 worker nodes in prod EKS cluster showing NotReady status.
120 pods evicted and rescheduled. Payment service and order service impacted.
Customer-facing error rate: 12% (SLO breach – threshold 0.1%)

kubectl get nodes:
prod-node-07   NotReady   worker   2d    v1.28.3
prod-node-09   NotReady   worker   2d    v1.28.3
prod-node-11   NotReady   worker   2d    v1.28.3

Deep Investigation (L3):
1. journalctl -u kubelet on affected nodes:
   "Failed to get node info: nodes 'prod-node-07' not found"
   "PLEG is not healthy: pleg was last seen active 3m45s ago; threshold is 3m"
   PLEG = Pod Lifecycle Event Generator — kubelet's heartbeat mechanism

2. Root Cause: containerd runtime on nodes entered degraded state after OOM killer
   triggered at 14:19 IST killing containerd-shim processes.
   
   OOM trigger: Node memory pressure due to Datadog agent memory leak (v7.47.0 — known CVE)
   Datadog agent consuming 4.2GB RAM per node (expected: 250MB)
   
3. containerd degraded → kubelet PLEG unhealthy → node NotReady → mass pod eviction

Remediation:
1. Cordoned affected nodes: kubectl cordon prod-node-07/09/11
2. Drained gracefully: kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
3. Restarted containerd: systemctl restart containerd
4. Restarted kubelet: systemctl restart kubelet
5. Nodes back Ready within 4 minutes
6. Downgraded Datadog agent to v7.46.0 on all nodes (v7.47.0 has memory leak bug — vendor confirmed)
7. Uncordoned nodes. All pods rescheduled.

Total Impact: 23 minutes degradation. Error rate normalised at 14:45 IST.

RCA Report will follow within 24 hours.

Fish John
L3 Kubernetes Platform Engineer

subject: [L3-TKT-3002] Deep Dive – GitHub Actions OIDC auth failing intermittently with AWS (race condition)
body:
Hi Platform Engineering,

Ticket: L3-TKT-3002 | Priority: High | Component: GitHub Actions + AWS OIDC
Environment: Production CI/CD | Duration: Intermittent for 3 weeks

Background:
GitHub Actions workflows using OIDC (OpenID Connect) to assume AWS IAM roles are failing intermittently.
Failure rate: ~8% of runs. Retrying the job usually succeeds.
L1 and L2 teams marked as 'flaky network' and closed 3 times. Escalated to L3 for root cause.

Error:
Error: Could not assume role with OIDC: Not authorized to perform sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity
Status Code: 403

L3 Deep Investigation:
1. Collected 200 failure timestamps over 3 weeks.
2. Correlated with GitHub's OIDC token issuance service status — no outages found.
3. Examined JWT tokens in failed runs (decoded from GitHub Actions debug logs):
   Failed tokens: 'iat' (issued-at) timestamp was AHEAD of AWS STS server time by 6-10 seconds
   AWS STS rejects tokens where 'iat' > server_time (clock skew protection)
   
4. Root Cause: GitHub Actions runner pool has 3 runners with NTP drift.
   Two specific runner IPs (10.4.5.23, 10.4.5.31) — self-hosted runners — clock drifted +8 seconds
   GitHub-hosted runners unaffected.
   
5. AWS IAM role trust policy had no clock skew tolerance configured.

Fix:
1. Synced NTP on self-hosted runners: chronyc makestep
2. Added AWS IAM role condition: "aws:TokenIssueTime" with 30-second tolerance window
3. Configured chrony with redundant NTP servers (was using single internal NTP that also drifted)
4. Added monitoring: alert if NTP offset > 2 seconds on any runner

Failure rate post-fix: 0% over 48 hours (200+ runs tested)

This was a classic distributed systems clock skew issue — rare but completely reproducible once understood.

Fish John
L3 Platform Security / CI Engineer

subject: [L3-TKT-3003] Memory leak in custom Jenkins agent causing OOM after 72h – JVM heap analysis
body:
Hi Engineering Leadership,

Ticket: L3-TKT-3003 | Priority: High | Component: Jenkins Custom Build Agent (Java)

Problem:
Custom Jenkins build agent (acme-build-agent:v2.3) crashes every 72-80 hours with:
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space

The agent runs as a containerised JVM process. After crash, all in-progress builds fail.
This has occurred 4 times in the past 3 weeks.

L3 JVM Heap Analysis:
1. Enabled JVM heap dump on OOM: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/dumps/
2. Analysed heap dump with Eclipse MAT (Memory Analyser Tool)
   Retained Heap: 3.8GB (JVM max: 4GB)
   
3. Dominator tree showed:
   - org.apache.groovy.parser.antlr4.GroovyLangParser retained 2.1GB
   - 847,293 instances of SyntaxErrorMessage objects never garbage collected
   
4. Root Cause: Groovy parser in Jenkins plugin 'pipeline-groovy-lib:689.vf91' has a known memory leak
   when parsing Groovy scripts with syntax errors — SyntaxErrorMessage objects accumulate in
   a static cache that is never cleared.
   
   Bug reference: JENKINS-71234 (known issue, fixed in plugin version 700.v796)

Fix:
1. Upgraded pipeline-groovy-lib plugin: 689.vf91 → 700.v796
2. Set JVM GC tuning: -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=16m
3. Added liveness probe to restart agent if heap > 80%: livenessProbe.exec: jstat check
4. 96 hours post-fix: heap stable at 800MB. No OOM events.

Full heap analysis report attached: heap-analysis-L3-3003.pdf

Fish John
L3 JVM Performance Engineer

subject: [L4-TKT-4001] VENDOR ESCALATION – EKS Control Plane API server latency spike affecting all deployments
body:
Hi AWS Enterprise Support + Architecture Team,

Ticket: L4-TKT-4001 | Priority: Critical | Component: AWS EKS Control Plane
Case: AWS Support Case #1234567890

Issue:
AWS EKS control plane API server experiencing intermittent latency spikes for the past 5 days.
kubectl commands timing out: "Error from server: etcd cluster is unavailable" (intermittent)
API server p99 latency: 12,000ms (normal: <100ms)

All deployments, scaling events and helm upgrades are affected during spike windows (15-20 min bursts, 3-4x daily).

Our Investigation (exhausted all internal options):
1. Worker nodes healthy — issue isolated to control plane (AWS managed)
2. API audit logs show 50,000+ LIST requests/second from Flux CD controllers during spike windows
3. Flux CD performing full resource reconciliation loop every 60 seconds across 340 CRDs
4. This LIST storm overwhelming etcd under the control plane

AWS Response (Case #1234567890):
AWS confirmed: EKS control plane etcd has a known throttling issue when LIST request rate
exceeds 40,000/s from a single cluster. Fix deployed in EKS 1.29 patch (not yet available for our 1.28).

Architectural Decision Required (L4):
Option A: Upgrade EKS from 1.28 → 1.29 immediately (includes etcd fix)
           Risk: In-place control plane upgrade, 30-min maintenance window
Option B: Configure Flux CD reconciliation interval from 60s → 300s to reduce LIST pressure
           Risk: 5-minute delay in GitOps sync (acceptable?)
Option C: Shard Flux CD into 2 controllers across separate namespaces
           Risk: Operational complexity increase

Recommendation: Option B immediately + Option A scheduled for next maintenance window.

Requires CTO approval for EKS version upgrade decision.

Fish John
L4 Platform Architect | AWS Certified Solutions Architect

subject: [L4-TKT-4002] Architectural Review – HashiCorp Vault secret sprawl causing pipeline bottleneck at scale
body:
Hi Architecture Board,

Ticket: L4-TKT-4002 | Priority: High | Component: HashiCorp Vault + CI/CD Secret Management

Context:
We now have 340 microservices, each with 15-30 secrets in Vault.
Total: ~8,500 individual secrets managed in Vault.
CI/CD pipelines retrieve secrets at build time using Vault Agent Injector.

Problem at Scale:
1. Pipeline secret retrieval latency increased from 800ms → 8,400ms over 6 months
2. Vault audit log: 45,000 secret lease requests/hour during peak CI time (9-11 AM)
3. Vault cluster CPU: 85-90% during peak (3-node cluster, each 8 vCPU)
4. Vault token renewal storms: 340 services renewing tokens simultaneously on the hour

Root Architecture Issue:
Current design: Every pipeline run does a full Vault login → token fetch → secret read for ALL secrets
This is a Vault anti-pattern at this scale.

L4 Architectural Solutions Considered:

Option 1: Vault Namespace Sharding
- Split into 4 Vault namespaces by domain (payments, orders, auth, infra)
- Reduces per-namespace load by 75%
- Effort: 3 sprints

Option 2: Secret Caching with External Secrets Operator
- ESO syncs Vault secrets to Kubernetes Secrets (cached in etcd)
- Pipelines read from K8s Secrets — no live Vault calls during builds
- Reduces Vault load by 90%
- Effort: 2 sprints (recommended)

Option 3: Vault Enterprise Performance Replication
- Adds read replicas to distribute load
- Cost: $85,000/year Vault Enterprise licence
- Effort: 1 sprint

Recommendation: Option 2 (External Secrets Operator) — best cost/performance ratio.
Detailed migration plan attached: vault-eso-migration-plan-v2.pdf

Decision needed from: CTO + Security Architect

Fish John
L4 Secrets Management Architect

subject: [INSTALL] Trivy + Trivy Operator – Container Security Scanning in Kubernetes
body:
Hi Security & DevOps Team,

Installation Request: INST-2024-048
Tool: Trivy v0.47.0 + Trivy Operator v0.18.0
Purpose: Automated container image vulnerability scanning in CI pipeline and Kubernetes cluster
Compliance: SOC2 Type II requirement — all container images must be scanned before deployment

Installation Summary:

Phase 1 – Trivy CLI in Jenkins Pipeline:
Added Trivy scanning step to all Jenkins pipelines:
stage('Security Scan') {

}

Operator installed in namespace 'trivy-system'
CRDs created: VulnerabilityReport, ConfigAuditReport, ExposedSecretReport, RbacAssessmentReport

- HIGH CVEs: 187 (across 34 images) — sprint tickets created

subject: [INSTALL] Atlantis – Terraform Pull Request Automation Setup
body:
Hi Platform Team,

Installation Request: INST-2024-052
Tool: Atlantis v0.26.0
Purpose: Automate Terraform plan/apply via GitHub Pull Requests
Benefit: No more manual terraform apply from laptops — all infra changes via PR workflow with audit trail

Architecture:
Atlantis server deployed as Kubernetes Deployment in 'atlantis' namespace.
Webhook configured in all 8 Terraform GitHub repositories.

Installation:

1. Atlantis Deployment (Kubernetes):
   helm install atlantis runatlantis/atlantis -n atlantis -f atlantis-values.yaml
   Key configs in atlantis-values.yaml:
   - orgAllowlist: github.com/acme-corp/*
   - requireApproval: true
   - requireMergeable: true
   - workflowMode: per-dir

2. GitHub Webhook (all 8 repos):
   Events: pull_request, issue_comment
   Secret: stored in Kubernetes secret (not in YAML)

3. AWS IAM Role for Atlantis:
   Created IAM role 'atlantis-terraform-role' with least privilege
   IRSA configured (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)

Workflow Now Live:
Developer opens PR → Atlantis comments: terraform plan output automatically
Reviewer approves PR → Comments 'atlantis apply' → Terraform applies automatically
Full audit trail in GitHub PR comments.

First week results:
- 34 Terraform PRs processed automatically
- 0 manual terraform apply commands (verified via AWS CloudTrail)
- 2 dangerous destroys caught in plan review before apply

Fish John
L3 Infrastructure Automation
